enrichment.short_description: Mouseflow is a website behavior analytics platform that combines session replay, heatmaps, funnels, and form analytics to help teams find and fix friction in the user experience.
enrichment.seo_title: Mouseflow Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Mouseflow review 2026: see how this Copenhagen-built heatmap and session replay tool prices, what it tracks, and how it compares to Hotjar and Crazy Egg.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Mouseflow is a behavior analytics and conversion rate optimization platform founded in Denmark by Lasse Schou Holboll (Crunchbase lists the founding year as 2009, though some Mouseflow materials reference 2010). The company is headquartered in Copenhagen, Denmark, and by 2025 reported serving more than 190,000 digital experience professionals across marketing, product, UX, analytics, and CRO teams worldwide, with roughly 60 employees spread across Europe, North America, and Asia. Mouseflow was reported as acquired in August 2025. The product works by embedding a lightweight JavaScript tracking snippet on a website, which then records anonymized visitor sessions and aggregates interaction data into heatmaps and analytics dashboards, requiring no code changes to implement. Core capabilities include session replay (video-like playback of real visitor sessions), five heatmap types (click, scroll, movement, attention, and geographical), conversion funnel analysis, user journey mapping, form analytics that show field-level abandonment and completion rates, a proprietary Friction Score that automatically flags sessions where users struggled (rage clicks, dead clicks, error clicks, or excessive scrolling), and on-page feedback surveys. Higher tiers add an open REST API, MCP (Model Context Protocol) connectivity for AI workflows, single sign-on, and dedicated customer success support. Pricing follows a freemium model: a permanent Free plan covers 500 sessions per month on one website with one conversion funnel and one month of data retention; paid plans start with Essential at 25 per month (5,000 sessions, one project, three months retention), scaling through Advanced at 109 per month (25,000 sessions, form and journey analytics, feedback surveys) and Premium at 319 per month (100,000 sessions, API access, MCP, SSO), up to a custom-priced Enterprise tier with unlimited sessions and a dedicated SLA. Annual billing is discounted versus monthly. Mouseflow's target audience spans small business marketers, ecommerce teams, product/UX researchers, and CRO agencies who need to understand why visitors abandon carts, forms, or signup flows rather than just what pages they viewed. Its primary competitors are Hotjar, Crazy Egg, FullStory, Contentsquare, and Microsoft Clarity (a free alternative). Mouseflow differentiates on its combination of comprehensive heatmap types plus session replay plus the Friction Score in a single unified platform, its GDPR-oriented data handling for the EU market given its Danish base, and depth of form-analytics tooling relative to lower-cost competitors like Clarity.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Mouseflow is a Copenhagen-based behavior analytics tool that helps website owners see exactly how visitors interact with their pages. Instead of relying only on aggregate traffic numbers from tools like Google Analytics, Mouseflow captures individual visitor sessions and turns them into replayable videos, heatmaps, and funnel reports.

The platform is aimed at marketing, product, UX, and conversion rate optimization teams who need to understand friction points such as confusing navigation, broken form fields, or ignored calls to action. Mouseflow has been in the behavior analytics space since the late 2000s and reports serving well over 100,000 sites globally.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At the core of Mouseflow is session replay, which lets teams watch anonymized recordings of real visitor sessions, including mouse movement, clicks, scrolling, and form interaction. This is paired with five heatmap types (click, scroll, movement, attention, and geographical) that visualize aggregate behavior across many sessions at once.

Mouseflow also includes a Friction Score that automatically surfaces sessions with signs of user frustration, such as rage clicks or repeated form errors, so teams do not have to manually sift through recordings. Conversion funnels, user journey analysis, form analytics, and on-site feedback surveys round out the toolkit, and higher-tier plans add API and MCP access for integrating behavior data into other systems.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Mouseflow uses a freemium pricing model. The Free plan is permanent and includes 500 monthly sessions, one website project, and one month of data retention, making it usable for very small sites to test the product.

Paid plans start at Essential (25 per month) and scale through Advanced (109 per month, the most popular tier) and Premium (319 per month) based on monthly session volume, number of tracked websites, data retention length, and access to advanced features like form analytics, journey analytics, and API access. An Enterprise tier offers custom pricing with unlimited sessions and dedicated support.

features.title: Session Replay
features.description: Watch anonymized video-like recordings of real visitor sessions, including clicks, scrolling, and mouse movement.

features.title: Heatmaps
features.description: Click, scroll, movement, attention, and geographical heatmaps visualize aggregate visitor behavior across pages.

features.title: Friction Score
features.description: An automated scoring system flags sessions with signs of user frustration such as rage clicks and form errors.

features.title: Conversion Funnels
features.description: Track where visitors drop off across multi-step flows like checkout or signup.

features.title: Form Analytics
features.description: Field-level insights into which form fields cause hesitation, errors, or abandonment.

features.title: User Journey Analysis
features.description: Visualize the paths visitors take through a site before converting or leaving.

features.title: Feedback Surveys
features.description: On-page surveys collect direct visitor feedback alongside behavioral data.

features.title: Open API and MCP Connectivity
features.description: Premium and Enterprise plans expose a REST API and MCP support for integrating session data into other tools and AI workflows.

pros: Combines session replay, heatmaps, funnels, and form analytics in one platform rather than requiring separate tools
Permanent free plan makes it accessible for small sites and testing before committing to a paid tier
Friction Score helps teams prioritize which sessions to review instead of watching recordings at random
Denmark-based with GDPR-conscious data handling, appealing to EU-focused businesses
Detailed form analytics that go deeper than many lower-cost competitors
cons: Session and data retention limits on lower tiers can be restrictive for higher-traffic sites
Advanced features like API access and SSO are reserved for the more expensive Premium and Enterprise tiers
Session-based pricing means costs can rise quickly as traffic grows
Some users report a learning curve to fully use funnel and journey analytics features
Free plan's one-month data retention is short for tracking longer-term trends

faqs.question: Is Mouseflow free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Mouseflow offers a permanent Free plan covering 500 monthly sessions on one website, though paid plans are needed for higher traffic volumes, more projects, and advanced features.

faqs.question: What is Mouseflow's Friction Score?
faqs.answer: Friction Score is Mouseflow's proprietary metric that automatically flags visitor sessions showing signs of frustration, such as rage clicks, dead clicks, or repeated form errors, so teams can prioritize which recordings to review.

faqs.question: Where is Mouseflow based?
faqs.answer: Mouseflow is headquartered in Copenhagen, Denmark, and was founded by Lasse Schou Holboll.

faqs.question: How does Mouseflow compare to Hotjar?
faqs.answer: Both offer heatmaps and session replay, but Mouseflow is known for its broader set of heatmap types and deeper form analytics, while Hotjar is often noted for its simpler onboarding and survey tooling.

faqs.question: Does Mouseflow slow down my website?
faqs.answer: Mouseflow uses an asynchronous JavaScript tracking snippet designed to load without blocking page rendering, similar to other analytics tags, though any third-party script adds some overhead.

faqs.question: Can Mouseflow be used for GDPR-compliant tracking?
faqs.answer: Mouseflow provides privacy controls such as data masking for sensitive form fields and is positioned for GDPR-conscious use given its EU base, though compliance ultimately depends on how a site configures and discloses tracking.

use_cases.title: Ecommerce Conversion Optimization
use_cases.description: Online stores use Mouseflow's funnels and session replay to identify where shoppers abandon checkout and fix friction in the purchase flow.

use_cases.title: SaaS Onboarding Analysis
use_cases.description: Product teams review session recordings and form analytics to see where new users get stuck during signup or trial onboarding.

use_cases.title: Landing Page Testing
use_cases.description: Marketing teams use heatmaps and feedback surveys to understand engagement with campaign landing pages before and after design changes.

features: Form analytics, journey analytics, feedback surveys
Open REST API, MCP connectivity, SSO, priority support

enrichment.short_description: Movim is a free, open-source, decentralized social network and web-based XMPP client that lets people chat, publish posts, and join communities across federated servers.
enrichment.seo_title: Movim Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Movim review 2026: how this open-source, decentralized XMPP social network and webmail platform works, its features, and how it compares to alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Movim, short for My Open Virtual Identity Manager, is a free and open-source decentralized social network and webmail platform built entirely on top of the XMPP (Extensible Messaging and Presence Protocol) open standard. The project was founded by developer Timothee Jaussoin in 2010 and is maintained by a small community of contributors, with Christine Ho noted as a co-maintainer; it is licensed under AGPL-3.0-or-later and hosted primarily on the movim.eu domain and GitHub. Architecturally, Movim is not itself an XMPP server; it is a responsive web client and application layer that connects to existing XMPP servers such as ejabberd or Prosody, meaning users need an XMPP/Jabber account (either self-hosted or from a public provider) to use it. Because it speaks the standard XMPP protocol, Movim interoperates with other XMPP clients such as Conversations, Pidgin, Xabber, and Gajim, and its official public instance runs at movim.eu alongside numerous independently operated public instances. Core features include one-to-one and group chat, video conferencing, OMEMO end-to-end encrypted messaging, a Markdown-based blogging and publication system for posting to a federated timeline, automatic link and image embedding, and topic-based Communities that function similarly to forums or subreddits but are federated across servers rather than centralized. There is no cloud SaaS product to subscribe to and no paid tier; the software is entirely free to self-host or to use via a public instance, funded through donations and volunteer development rather than commercial revenue. Movim's target audience is privacy-conscious users, decentralization advocates, and existing XMPP/Jabber communities who want a modern, social-network-style interface layered on top of federated messaging infrastructure, as an alternative to closed platforms like Facebook or Twitter/X. Its closest competitors and comparables are other federated/open social platforms such as Mastodon and Diaspora, and other XMPP web clients such as Converse.js and Snikket, though Movim differentiates itself by combining chat, blogging, and social networking in a single client built natively for the XMPP ecosystem rather than ActivityPub.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Movim is an open-source project that turns the decades-old XMPP messaging protocol into the backbone of a modern, decentralized social network. Rather than running its own centralized servers the way commercial social platforms do, Movim is a client application that anyone can self-host or access through one of many independently run public instances.

Founded by Timothee Jaussoin in 2010, Movim has grown into a mature community project offering chat, group video calls, blogging, and topic-based communities, all federated across the wider XMPP network so that Movim users can also communicate with people on entirely different XMPP clients and servers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Movim supports one-to-one and group messaging, video conferencing, and OMEMO end-to-end encryption for private conversations. Its social layer lets users publish Markdown-formatted posts to a personal timeline, with automatic embedding of shared links and images, similar to a federated blog.

Communities in Movim function like topic-focused public spaces that anyone can join or create, and because the whole platform runs on the XMPP protocol, it interoperates with a large ecosystem of existing XMPP clients and servers such as ejabberd and Prosody, rather than being a walled garden.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Movim is completely free and open source, released under the AGPL-3.0-or-later license. There is no paid tier or subscription; anyone can self-host their own instance at no software cost or create a free account on one of the public community-run instances such as the official movim.eu.
Because the project is community-maintained rather than a commercial company, ongoing development is funded through donations and volunteer contributions rather than product revenue.

features.title: XMPP-Based Messaging
features.description: One-to-one and group chat built on the open XMPP protocol, interoperable with other XMPP clients.

features.title: OMEMO Encryption
features.description: End-to-end encrypted conversations protect private messages between contacts.

features.title: Video Conferencing
features.description: Built-in support for video calls directly within the web client.

features.title: Blogging and Publications
features.description: Markdown-based posting lets users publish articles and updates to a federated timeline.

features.title: Communities
features.description: Topic-based public spaces that function like federated forums users can join or create.

features.title: Responsive Web Client
features.description: A cross-platform, mobile-friendly web interface that requires no separate app installation.

features.title: Federation and Interoperability
features.description: Runs on any compatible XMPP server and can communicate with users on other XMPP clients.

features.title: Self-Hosting Support
features.description: The full platform can be self-hosted for organizations or individuals wanting complete control over their data.

pros: Fully free and open source with no subscription cost
Decentralized architecture avoids single-company control over user data
Interoperates with the broader XMPP client and server ecosystem
Combines chat, blogging, and community features in a single platform
End-to-end encryption available via OMEMO for private conversations
cons: Requires an XMPP account, which adds setup friction for users unfamiliar with the protocol
Smaller network effect compared to mainstream social platforms
No official commercial support or SLA since it is a volunteer-maintained project
Interface and feature set can feel less polished than large venture-funded social apps
Server choice and instance quality vary since it is a federated, community-run ecosystem

faqs.question: Is Movim free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Movim is entirely free and open source under the AGPL-3.0-or-later license, with no paid plans.

faqs.question: Do I need a special account to use Movim?
faqs.answer: Yes, Movim requires an XMPP/Jabber account, which can be created on a public XMPP server or a Movim public instance such as join.movim.eu.

faqs.question: Is Movim a chat app or a social network?
faqs.answer: Both. Movim combines XMPP-based chat and video calls with a blogging and Communities system, functioning as a federated social network built on messaging infrastructure.

faqs.question: Can Movim users talk to people on other XMPP clients?
faqs.answer: Yes, because Movim uses the standard XMPP protocol, its users can communicate with people using other XMPP clients like Conversations, Pidgin, or Gajim.

faqs.question: Who maintains Movim?
faqs.answer: Movim was founded by developer Timothee Jaussoin in 2010 and continues to be maintained by a small community of open-source contributors.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Movim?
faqs.answer: Yes, Movim's source code is publicly available and organizations or individuals can self-host their own instance connected to an XMPP server such as ejabberd or Prosody.

use_cases.title: Privacy-Focused Social Networking
use_cases.description: Individuals who want to avoid centralized social platforms use Movim to chat and publish posts on a federated, self-hostable network.

use_cases.title: Community and Organization Messaging
use_cases.description: Groups and organizations self-host Movim to provide members with chat, video calls, and an internal blogging space under their own control.

use_cases.title: XMPP Ecosystem Access
use_cases.description: Existing Jabber/XMPP users adopt Movim as a modern web client to access their existing account with a richer social interface.
use_cases.audience: Existing XMPP and Jabber server users

features: Full access to chat, video, blogging, and Communities

enrichment.short_description: Mox is a modern, all-in-one open-source mail server written in Go that bundles SMTP, IMAP4, webmail, and built-in spam and authentication protections into a single low-maintenance binary.
enrichment.seo_title: Mox Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Mox review 2026: see how this Go-based, all-in-one open-source mail server handles SMTP, IMAP, webmail, spam filtering, and self-hosted email setup.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Mox is an open-source, all-in-one email server created by Dutch developer Mechiel Lukkien, with work on the project beginning around 2021. It is written in Go, a language chosen specifically to reduce memory-safety bugs common in older mail server codebases, and is released under the MIT license (incorporating some BSD-3-Clause code from the Go Authors and the Mozilla Public License-licensed Public Suffix List). Mox has received financial support from the NLnet Foundation and its NGI Zero programs (funded in part by the European Commission's Next Generation Internet initiative), including grants under NGI0 Entrust and NGI0 Core covering 2023 through early 2026, as well as support from the Netherlands Ministry of the Interior and Kingdom Relations, reflecting its status as a publicly-funded infrastructure project rather than a venture-backed company. Architecturally, Mox is unusual for combining functionality that is normally split across several separate daemons, an SMTP server for receiving, submitting, and delivering mail, a full IMAP4rev2 server for client access, and a built-in webmail interface, into one self-contained binary with no external dependencies beyond a database file it manages itself. It implements the modern authentication and delivery-security stack out of the box, including SPF, DKIM, and DMARC (with DMARC aggregate reporting), MTA-STS and DANE for encrypted delivery, TLS reporting (TLSRPT), and automatic TLS certificate provisioning via ACME/Let's Encrypt. For spam control, Mox uses per-account reputation tracking based on prior Junk/Non-Junk classification, Bayesian spam filtering that learns from user feedback, and connection throttling for first-time senders as an alternative to traditional greylisting. Administration is handled through a web-based admin interface for managing domains and accounts and reviewing DMARC/TLS reports, and Mox exposes a webhook and HTTP/JSON API for transactional email use cases, along with Prometheus metrics for monitoring. Setup is designed to be fast: an operator downloads the binary, runs a quickstart command, adds the DNS records it prints, and has a working mail server, positioning Mox against the traditional complexity of configuring Postfix, Dovecot, OpenDKIM, and SpamAssassin separately. Its target audience is technically capable individuals and small organizations who want to self-host email without maintaining a sprawling stack of interdependent daemons. Its closest competitors and comparison points are other self-hosted mail server projects such as Postfix plus Dovecot, Mailcow, Maddy, and Mail-in-a-Box, with Mox differentiating primarily through its single-binary Go implementation, security-by-default protocol support, and low ongoing maintenance burden.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Mox is an open-source mail server designed to make self-hosted email realistic for individuals and small teams by collapsing the traditionally fragmented mail server stack into a single binary. Where a typical self-hosted setup requires separately configuring an SMTP daemon, an IMAP daemon, spam filtering, and DKIM signing, Mox implements all of these in one Go program.

Created by developer Mechiel Lukkien with work starting around 2021 and supported by grants from the NLnet Foundation's NGI Zero programs, Mox is positioned as a modern alternative to legacy mail server software, prioritizing security defaults and low operational overhead.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Mox handles the full email lifecycle: SMTP for receiving, submitting, and relaying mail, IMAP4rev2 for client access, and a built-in webmail client for reading and composing messages directly in the browser. It implements SPF, DKIM, and DMARC authentication along with DMARC aggregate reporting, plus MTA-STS, DANE, and TLSRPT for more secure, verifiable delivery.

Spam defenses include per-account reputation tracking, Bayesian filtering that learns from how users classify Junk and Non-Junk mail, and connection slowdowns for first-time senders. Operators get a web-based admin interface, Prometheus metrics, structured logging, and automatic TLS certificate issuance via ACME.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Mox is free, open-source software licensed under the MIT license, with no commercial tiers or subscription costs. Anyone can download the binary and self-host it on their own server.

Development is funded through public grants, primarily from the NLnet Foundation's NGI Zero programs and related European Commission Next Generation Internet initiatives, rather than through product sales.

features.title: All-in-One Mail Server
features.description: Combines SMTP, IMAP4rev2, and webmail into a single self-contained binary with no external service dependencies.

features.title: Built-In Webmail
features.description: A browser-based email client with threading, keyboard shortcuts, and drag-and-drop support is included out of the box.

features.title: Email Authentication
features.description: Native SPF, DKIM, and DMARC support with DMARC aggregate report handling to authenticate outgoing and incoming mail.

features.title: Secure Delivery Protocols
features.description: Supports MTA-STS, DANE, and REQUIRETLS for encrypted mail delivery, plus TLS reporting.

features.title: Spam and Reputation Filtering
features.description: Per-account reputation tracking and Bayesian spam filtering learn from user Junk/Non-Junk classifications.

features.title: Automatic TLS
features.description: Certificates are automatically provisioned and renewed via ACME and Let's Encrypt.

features.title: Admin Web Interface
features.description: A web UI for managing domains, accounts, and reviewing DMARC and TLS reports.

features.title: Quickstart Setup
features.description: A guided quickstart command generates the required DNS records for a fast initial deployment.

pros: Single binary eliminates the need to configure and maintain several separate mail daemons
Modern security protocols like MTA-STS, DANE, and DMARC are supported out of the box
Written in Go, reducing the risk of memory-safety bugs common in older mail server code
Built-in webmail means no separate client software is required to read or send mail
Backed by public NLnet/NGI Zero funding rather than dependent on a single company's survival
cons: Younger project than established mail servers like Postfix or Dovecot, with a smaller community and fewer third-party guides
Self-hosting email in general requires ongoing attention to deliverability and IP reputation regardless of software
No official managed hosting or paid support plan for non-technical users
Smaller plugin/extension ecosystem compared to decades-old mail server stacks
Requires comfort with DNS configuration and server administration to deploy correctly

faqs.question: Is Mox free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Mox is free, open-source software released under the MIT license with no paid tiers.

faqs.question: Who created Mox?
faqs.answer: Mox was created by developer Mechiel Lukkien, with development supported by grants from the NLnet Foundation's NGI Zero programs.

faqs.question: What protocols does Mox support?
faqs.answer: Mox implements SMTP, IMAP4rev2, SPF, DKIM, DMARC, MTA-STS, DANE, TLSRPT, and automatic TLS via ACME, along with a built-in webmail interface.

faqs.question: Does Mox include a webmail client?
faqs.answer: Yes, Mox ships with a built-in browser-based webmail client for reading and sending email without needing a separate mail app.

faqs.question: How is Mox different from Postfix and Dovecot?
faqs.answer: Postfix and Dovecot are separate SMTP and IMAP daemons that must be configured and connected together, while Mox combines equivalent SMTP, IMAP, and webmail functionality into a single Go binary.

faqs.question: What language is Mox written in?
faqs.answer: Mox is written in Go, chosen for its memory safety and suitability for building a secure, low-maintenance server application.

use_cases.title: Self-Hosted Personal Email
use_cases.description: Individuals who want full control over their email run Mox on a personal server instead of relying on a third-party mailbox provider.

use_cases.title: Small Organization Mail Hosting
use_cases.description: Small teams or nonprofits self-host Mox to handle domain email without paying for per-mailbox commercial hosting.

use_cases.title: Low-Maintenance Mail Infrastructure
use_cases.description: System administrators who want modern security defaults without manually stitching together SPF, DKIM, DMARC, and spam filtering tools choose Mox to reduce operational overhead.

features: Full SMTP, IMAP4rev2, and webmail functionality
Built-in spam filtering and email authentication
No licensing fees, community and grant-funded development

enrichment.short_description: Moz Pro is Moz's core SEO software suite, covering keyword research, rank tracking, site audits, backlink analysis, and competitive research in one platform.
enrichment.seo_title: Moz Pro Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Moz Pro review 2026: pricing plans, keyword research, rank tracking, and site audit features from the Seattle-based SEO platform, plus top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Moz Pro is the flagship SEO software suite from Moz, a Seattle, Washington-based company originally founded in 2004 by Rand Fishkin and his mother Gillian Muessig as SEOmoz, initially a blog and SEO consulting community. The company transitioned from consulting to proprietary software, raised roughly $29 million in venture funding across multiple rounds, and rebranded from SEOmoz to Moz in May 2013. Rand Fishkin stepped down as CEO in January 2014 and was succeeded by Sarah Bird; Moz has since gone through additional leadership changes and today operates as an independent SEO software company with roughly 300 employees. Moz Pro is a cloud-based, browser-accessed SaaS platform (no self-hosted option) that bundles the tools SEO practitioners need for organic search strategy: keyword research and keyword suggestion tools, rank tracking across search engines and locations, on-demand and scheduled site audits that crawl a domain for technical SEO issues, backlink analysis powered by Moz's proprietary Link Explorer index, competitive research features to benchmark against rival domains, and the MozBar browser extension for on-page SEO diagnostics. Moz is also known for popularizing widely cited proprietary metrics, Domain Authority (DA) and Page Authority (PA), which estimate a website's or page's likely ranking strength based on its link profile, and more recently Brand Authority. Moz Pro's pricing is tiered by usage: the Starter plan runs from about 39 per month billed annually (or 49 monthly) and includes 50 tracked keywords and 20,000 crawled pages; Standard around 99 per month adds backlink analysis, multiple sites, and more keyword tracking; Medium around 179 per month is Moz's most popular tier, supporting multiple users, up to 10 tracked websites, and higher keyword and crawl limits; and Large at roughly 299 per month offers the highest limits for agencies and larger in-house teams. All plans include a 7-day free trial and access to Moz's AI-assisted keyword and content tools. Moz Pro's target audience spans in-house marketing teams, SEO agencies, and freelance consultants who need an all-in-one toolkit rather than assembling point solutions. Its main competitors are Ahrefs, Semrush, and Ubersuggest, with Moz differentiating through its long-standing Domain Authority metric, its educational content and Whiteboard Friday legacy, and a UI generally regarded as more approachable for SEO beginners than some rivals.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Moz Pro is a cloud-based SEO toolkit built by Moz, one of the oldest and most recognized names in the search marketing industry. Originally launched in 2004 as SEOmoz, a blog and consulting practice founded by Rand Fishkin and Gillian Muessig, the company evolved into a subscription software business and rebranded to Moz in 2013.

Today Moz Pro brings together keyword research, rank tracking, site audits, backlink analysis, and competitive research in a single subscription platform, aimed at helping marketers plan, execute, and measure organic search strategy without stitching together multiple point tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Moz Pro's keyword research tools help identify and prioritize target terms using search volume and difficulty estimates, while rank tracking monitors how a site's pages perform in search results over time across chosen locations and devices. Site audits crawl a domain to surface technical SEO issues like broken links, duplicate content, and crawl errors.

The platform's backlink analysis, powered by Moz's proprietary Link Explorer index, shows a site's inbound link profile and that of competitors, and is closely tied to Moz's well-known Domain Authority and Page Authority scores. The MozBar browser extension extends these insights to on-page diagnostics while browsing any website.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Moz Pro is sold as a tiered monthly or annual subscription. The entry-level Starter plan costs about 39 per month billed annually (49 billed monthly) and covers 50 tracked keywords and 20,000 crawled pages, suitable for small sites or solo practitioners.

Higher tiers, Standard, Medium, and Large, scale up keyword tracking, number of sites, monthly crawled pages, and user seats, topping out around 299 per month for the Large plan. All plans include a 7-day free trial, and annual billing offers meaningful savings versus paying monthly.

features.title: Keyword Research
features.description: Keyword suggestion and search volume tools help identify and prioritize target search terms.

features.title: Rank Tracking
features.description: Monitors keyword rankings over time across chosen search engines, locations, and devices.

features.title: Site Audit
features.description: Crawls a domain to identify technical SEO issues such as broken links, duplicate content, and crawl errors.

features.title: Backlink Analysis (Link Explorer)
features.description: Analyzes a site's inbound link profile and competitors' links using Moz's proprietary link index.

features.title: Domain Authority and Page Authority
features.description: Moz's widely cited proprietary metrics estimate a site's or page's likely ranking strength based on its link profile.

features.title: Competitive Research
features.description: Benchmarks a domain's visibility, keywords, and backlinks against competitor sites.

features.title: MozBar Browser Extension
features.description: A free companion extension surfaces on-page SEO metrics and Domain Authority while browsing any website.

features.title: AI-Assisted Keyword and Content Tools
features.description: Included across all plans to help generate keyword ideas and content suggestions.

pros: All-in-one suite covers keyword research, rank tracking, audits, and backlinks without needing separate tools
Domain Authority and Page Authority are widely recognized, industry-standard-adjacent metrics
Generally considered more approachable for SEO beginners than some competitor interfaces
Long operating history and strong educational content library built over two decades
7-day free trial available on all plans before committing to a subscription
cons: Keyword and page crawl limits on lower tiers can be restrictive for larger sites
Backlink index and keyword database are often considered smaller than Ahrefs' by SEO professionals
Pricing rises quickly at higher tiers for agencies managing many client sites
Some advanced competitive research features are gated behind higher-priced plans
No self-hosted or one-time-purchase option; subscription-only

faqs.question: What is Moz Pro used for?
faqs.answer: Moz Pro is an SEO software suite used for keyword research, rank tracking, technical site audits, backlink analysis, and competitive research.

faqs.question: How much does Moz Pro cost?
faqs.answer: Moz Pro starts around 39 per month billed annually (49 billed monthly) on the Starter plan, scaling up through Standard, Medium, and Large tiers to about 299 per month.

faqs.question: Who founded Moz?
faqs.answer: Moz was founded in 2004 by Rand Fishkin and Gillian Muessig as SEOmoz, later rebranding to Moz in 2013.

faqs.question: What is Domain Authority?
faqs.answer: Domain Authority is a proprietary Moz metric that scores a website's likely ranking strength in search results based primarily on its backlink profile.

faqs.question: Does Moz Pro offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, Moz Pro plans include a 7-day free trial before requiring payment.

faqs.question: How does Moz Pro compare to Ahrefs and Semrush?
faqs.answer: Moz Pro is generally viewed as more beginner-friendly with a simpler interface, while Ahrefs and Semrush are often noted for larger backlink and keyword databases and deeper competitive research features.

use_cases.title: In-House SEO Management
use_cases.description: Marketing teams use Moz Pro to track rankings, run technical audits, and research keywords for their own company website.

enrichment.short_description: Music Player Daemon (MPD) is a free, open-source background music server that plays audio files and manages playlists, controlled remotely by a wide variety of separate client applications.
enrichment.seo_title: MPD (Music Player Daemon) Review 2026: Features and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: MPD review 2026: how the open-source Music Player Daemon works, its client/server architecture, supported formats, and how it compares to alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Music Player Daemon, universally known as MPD, is a free and open-source, server-side music playback application originally developed by Max Kellermann and first released in 2003. Rather than being a conventional desktop music player with its own interface, MPD is a background daemon: it runs on a machine (commonly a home server, NAS, or single-board computer like a Raspberry Pi), maintains a database of a user's music library, manages playlists, and handles audio playback, while exposing a documented network protocol over TCP that separate client applications use to control it remotely. This client/server architecture is MPD's defining characteristic and the reason it has remained relevant for over two decades: because the playback engine is decoupled from the interface, a large and varied ecosystem of clients has grown around it, ranging from the official bundled command-line client mpc, to graphical desktop clients such as Cantata and Euphonica, to mobile apps and web-based interfaces, all of which can control the same running MPD instance simultaneously. MPD is written in C++ and licensed under the GNU General Public License version 2 (GPLv2), with active development continuing under the MusicPlayerDaemon organization on GitHub. Technically, MPD supports playback of common formats including FLAC, Ogg Vorbis, MP3, and HTTP audio streams, reads embedded metadata such as ID3 tags, Vorbis Comments, and MP4 metadata, and offers features like gapless playback, crossfading, seeking, and M3U playlist management. For audio output, it has native plugin support for ALSA, PulseAudio, OSS, JACK, and other backends, making it flexible across Linux-centric home audio setups. MPD does not sell anything and has no company behind it; it is maintained as a community open-source project with no venture funding, no headquarters, and no paid tiers. Its target audience is technically inclined home audio enthusiasts and self-hosters who want a lightweight, always-on music server they can control from any device on their network, often as the playback backend for whole-home audio setups. Notably, other self-hosted audio projects such as moOde Audio and various NAS-based music servers are built as front-ends or distributions layered on top of MPD rather than being competitors to it. Its closer comparables as standalone self-hosted music servers are projects like mStream, Navidrome, and Logitech Media Server (Squeezebox), though MPD differentiates through its minimalist, protocol-first design and its decades-long track record as foundational infrastructure for other music software.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Music Player Daemon (MPD) is one of the longest-running open-source audio projects still in active use, first released by Max Kellermann in 2003. Unlike typical desktop music players, MPD has no built-in interface of its own; it runs quietly in the background as a server process, playing music and managing a library while other applications connect to it to provide the actual user interface.

This client/server design has made MPD a foundational piece of home audio infrastructure: it is commonly deployed on always-on machines like a Raspberry Pi, NAS, or home server, and controlled from phones, desktops, or web browsers via any of the dozens of compatible client applications built over the years.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: MPD plays a wide range of audio formats including FLAC, Ogg Vorbis, and MP3, and can stream internet radio over HTTP. It reads and caches file metadata such as ID3 tags and Vorbis Comments to build a searchable music database, and supports gapless playback, crossfading, and seeking for a polished listening experience.

Output flexibility is a core strength: MPD has native plugins for ALSA, PulseAudio, OSS, and JACK among others, letting it integrate with almost any Linux audio setup. Its network protocol lets multiple clients, from the bundled mpc command-line tool to graphical apps like Cantata, control the same MPD instance concurrently.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: MPD is entirely free and open-source software, licensed under the GNU General Public License version 2 (GPLv2). There is no commercial version, subscription, or paid tier of any kind.

As a community-maintained project with no company behind it, MPD is developed and supported by volunteer contributors under the MusicPlayerDaemon organization, with no revenue model beyond optional donations to individual contributors.

features.title: Client/Server Architecture
features.description: MPD runs as a background daemon that any number of separate client applications can connect to and control over the network.

features.title: Broad Format Support
features.description: Plays FLAC, Ogg Vorbis, MP3, and other common audio formats, plus HTTP audio streams.

features.title: Metadata Database
features.description: Reads and caches ID3, Vorbis Comment, and MP4 metadata to build a searchable, browsable music library.

features.title: Gapless Playback and Crossfading
features.description: Supports seamless track transitions for albums and DJ-style mixes.

features.title: Playlist Management
features.description: Save, load, and manage playlists in the standard M3U format.

features.title: Multiple Audio Output Plugins
features.description: Native support for ALSA, PulseAudio, OSS, and JACK audio backends.

features.title: Network Control Protocol
features.description: A documented TCP-based protocol lets any compatible client remotely control playback and the library.

features.title: Large Client Ecosystem
features.description: Dozens of official and community clients exist, from command-line tools to graphical desktop, mobile, and web interfaces.

pros: Extremely lightweight and stable, able to run reliably on low-power hardware like a Raspberry Pi
Decoupled client/server design allows controlling playback from any device on the network
Large, mature ecosystem of compatible client applications built over two decades
Free and open source with no subscription, ads, or vendor lock-in
Serves as trusted foundational infrastructure for other self-hosted audio projects
cons: No built-in graphical interface, requiring a separate client to actually use it
Initial setup and configuration can be intimidating for non-technical users
Lacks native streaming-service integration found in commercial music apps
Documentation quality varies across the many third-party clients
Feature set is intentionally minimal, so advanced library management may require client-side plugins

faqs.question: What is MPD used for?
faqs.answer: MPD (Music Player Daemon) is a background server that plays music from a local library and can be controlled remotely by separate client applications, commonly used for home audio setups.

faqs.question: Is MPD free?
faqs.answer: Yes, MPD is free and open-source software licensed under the GNU GPL version 2, with no paid version.

faqs.question: Do I need a client app to use MPD?
faqs.answer: Yes, since MPD has no built-in interface, you need a separate client such as mpc, Cantata, or a mobile app to control playback.

faqs.question: What audio formats does MPD support?
faqs.answer: MPD supports FLAC, Ogg Vorbis, MP3, and several other common formats, along with HTTP audio streaming.

faqs.question: Who created MPD?
faqs.answer: MPD was originally developed by Max Kellermann and first released in 2003, and continues to be maintained by the MusicPlayerDaemon open-source community.

faqs.question: Is MPD the same as moOde Audio?
faqs.answer: No, moOde Audio is a separate Raspberry Pi audio distribution that uses MPD as its underlying playback engine, adding its own web interface and features on top.

use_cases.title: Whole-Home Audio Server
use_cases.description: Users run MPD on an always-on home server or Raspberry Pi to stream a local music library to speakers controlled from any device.

use_cases.title: Headless Media Center Backend
use_cases.description: MPD serves as the audio playback engine for headless media setups, controlled remotely via mobile or web clients.
use_cases.audience: DIY home theater and media center builders

use_cases.title: Foundation for Other Audio Projects
use_cases.description: Developers build dedicated interfaces and distributions, such as moOde Audio, on top of MPD's playback engine rather than writing a player from scratch.

features: Full playback and library management engine
Network control protocol for any compatible client

enrichment.short_description: mStream is a free, open-source, self-hosted music streaming server with its own web and mobile apps that lets you stream your personal music collection from anywhere.
enrichment.seo_title: mStream Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: mStream review 2026: how this open-source, self-hosted music streaming server works, key features, setup, and how it compares to Navidrome and Plex.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: mStream is a free, open-source, self-hosted music streaming server that lets users access their personal music collection from any device through a built-in web player and dedicated mobile apps. The project, hosted primarily under the IrosTheBeggar GitHub account (associated with Iros Software) and at mstream.io, was created in 2015 and released under the GPL-3.0 license, with the GitHub repository having accumulated over 2,300 stars. Unlike commercial streaming services, mStream stores no music itself and pulls no catalog from a central company; it simply serves whatever audio files exist in folders on the machine it runs on, supporting formats including FLAC, MP3, WAV, OGG, Opus, AAC, M4A, and M4B. Architecturally, mStream is designed to be fast to deploy, its documentation advertises a roughly 30-second server setup, and it runs cross-platform on Windows, macOS, Linux, and FreeBSD, using the lightweight embedded database LokiJS rather than a heavier external database, plus libraries such as Symphonia and Lofty for audio metadata and processing. Its API is built to mirror the underlying folder structure directly, so users can browse and stream music even before a full library database has been built or scanned. Key features include gapless playback, a Milkdrop-based audio visualizer, playlist creation and sharing, drag-and-drop file uploading through the web interface, offline sync support for mobile apps, and built-in yt-dlp integration for pulling in audio. mStream also includes torrent client integration, supporting Transmission, Deluge, and qBittorrent, and a federation feature that lets independent mStream servers share specific directories with each other using invite tokens, an unusual capability that lets self-hosters loosely network their personal libraries. There are no paid tiers, subscriptions, or cloud-hosted version; the entire product is free software that users download and run on their own hardware, with official mobile apps available for iOS and Android alongside the browser-based player. mStream's target audience is technically comfortable music collectors and self-hosting enthusiasts who own large personal audio libraries (including lossless FLAC files) and want a private alternative to commercial streaming services like Spotify or Apple Music, with an emphasis on ownership, audio quality, and direct control of their files. Its closest competitors and comparables in the self-hosted space are Navidrome, Airsonic/Subsonic-compatible servers, and Music Player Daemon (MPD)-based setups, with mStream differentiating through its very fast setup process, built-in visualizer and torrent integration, and unique server-to-server federation feature.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: mStream is an open-source, self-hosted music streaming server built for people who want to access their own audio library from anywhere without handing their files or listening data to a commercial streaming company. Created in 2015 and maintained on GitHub under the IrosTheBeggar account, it has become one of the more widely used self-hosted personal streaming projects, with over 2,300 GitHub stars.

Rather than requiring a complex install process, mStream is designed around a fast setup, its own documentation cites roughly 30 seconds to get a server running, using a lightweight embedded database rather than a heavier external one, and running natively on Windows, macOS, Linux, and FreeBSD.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: mStream streams common lossy and lossless formats including FLAC, MP3, WAV, OGG, Opus, AAC, and M4A/M4B, and includes gapless playback plus a Milkdrop-style visualizer. Users can create and share playlists, upload files by dragging and dropping them into the web interface, and sync music for offline listening in the official iOS and Android apps.

Distinctive extras set mStream apart from many self-hosted alternatives: built-in yt-dlp support for importing audio, integration with torrent clients like Transmission, Deluge, and qBittorrent, and a federation feature that lets separate mStream servers share specific music directories with each other using invite tokens.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: mStream is completely free and open-source software, licensed under GPL-3.0, with no paid plans, subscriptions, or cloud-hosted product to buy. Users run it entirely on their own hardware, from a home computer to a NAS or VPS.
Because there is no commercial entity monetizing the product, mStream's ongoing development depends on its open-source community and maintainers rather than subscription revenue.

features.title: Fast Self-Hosted Setup
features.description: Documentation advertises roughly a 30-second server setup with no heavy external database required.

features.title: Wide Format Support
features.description: Streams FLAC, MP3, WAV, OGG, Opus, AAC, M4A, and M4B files directly from folders on the host machine.

features.title: Gapless Playback and Visualizer
features.description: Includes gapless track transitions and a Milkdrop-based audio visualizer in the web player.

features.title: Mobile Apps with Offline Sync
features.description: Official iOS and Android apps let users sync music for offline playback away from the home network.

features.title: Playlist Creation and Sharing
features.description: Users can build playlists and share them with other users or servers.

features.title: Torrent Client Integration
features.description: Connects to Transmission, Deluge, and qBittorrent to manage torrent-sourced media.

features.title: yt-dlp Integration
features.description: Built-in support for pulling audio via yt-dlp directly into the library.

features.title: Server Federation
features.description: Independent mStream servers can share specific directories with each other using invite tokens.

pros: Very fast, lightweight setup compared to more complex self-hosted media servers
Free and open source with no subscription cost or vendor lock-in
Supports lossless FLAC alongside common lossy formats for audiophile libraries
Unique federation feature allows sharing directories across independent servers
Cross-platform server support for Windows, macOS, Linux, and FreeBSD
cons: Smaller community and ecosystem compared to more established self-hosted media servers like Plex
No official managed hosting; users are responsible for their own server maintenance and uptime
Metadata and library organization tools are less advanced than dedicated media managers
Mobile app feature parity with the web client can lag behind updates
Torrent and yt-dlp integrations require additional configuration and awareness of legal considerations in some jurisdictions

faqs.question: Is mStream free?
faqs.answer: Yes, mStream is completely free and open-source software licensed under GPL-3.0, with no paid tiers.

faqs.question: What audio formats does mStream support?
faqs.answer: mStream supports FLAC, MP3, WAV, OGG, Opus, AAC, M4A, and M4B files.

faqs.question: Do I need special hardware to run mStream?
faqs.answer: No, mStream runs on Windows, macOS, Linux, and FreeBSD, and can be deployed on anything from a home computer to a NAS or VPS.

faqs.question: Does mStream have mobile apps?
faqs.answer: Yes, mStream offers official iOS and Android apps with offline sync support in addition to its browser-based web player.

faqs.question: What is mStream's federation feature?
faqs.answer: Federation lets independent mStream servers share specific music directories with each other using invite tokens, letting self-hosters loosely connect their libraries.

faqs.question: How is mStream different from MPD?
faqs.answer: mStream is a full self-hosted streaming server with its own web and mobile interfaces built in, while MPD (Music Player Daemon) is a background playback engine with no built-in interface that relies entirely on separate client applications.

use_cases.title: Personal Music Library Streaming
use_cases.description: Music collectors self-host mStream to stream their own FLAC and MP3 collections from any device, replacing commercial streaming subscriptions.

use_cases.title: Home NAS Media Server

use_cases.title: Federated Library Sharing
use_cases.description: Groups of friends or communities use mStream's federation feature to share specific music directories across independently run servers.

features: Full web player and mobile apps
Server federation and torrent client integration

enrichment.short_description: Multi Theft Auto: San Andreas (MTA:SA) is a free, open-source multiplayer mod that turns Grand Theft Auto: San Andreas into a networked, Lua-scriptable online game platform.
enrichment.seo_title: MTA:SA Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: MTA:SA review 2026: how Multi Theft Auto: San Andreas turns GTA: San Andreas into free open-source multiplayer, its Lua scripting, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Multi Theft Auto: San Andreas, known as MTA:SA, is a free, open-source multiplayer modification for Rockstar's 2004 game Grand Theft Auto: San Andreas, developed by the volunteer MTA Team. The broader Multi Theft Auto project traces its roots back to 2003, when founder known as IJsVogel released an early two-player multiplayer prototype for the original Grand Theft Auto III under the name GTA3AM (GTA III Alternative Multiplayer); as interest grew, a wider group of volunteer programmers and webmasters joined the effort. The project moved through GTA III and GTA: Vice City multiplayer versions before its San Andreas iteration began taking shape, with early playable San Andreas content unveiled in January 2006 and the project eventually being revised and relaunched as fully open-source software licensed under GPLv3, with its source code hosted publicly on GitHub under the multitheftauto organization. MTA:SA functions as a game engine layered on top of the single-player game, injecting networked multiplayer functionality, custom game modes, and a sandbox environment that server owners can extend using the Lua scripting language. This scripting layer is central to MTA:SA's longevity: it allows individual servers to implement wildly different experiences, from realistic roleplay servers and racing modes to deathmatch and freeroam, all running on the same core engine, similar in spirit to how Garry's Mod or Counter-Strike server modding works. Because it is entirely free, non-commercial, and open source, MTA:SA has no company behind it, no headquarters, and no pricing tiers; the software and all official servers are maintained by the volunteer MTA Team and community contributors. Despite being built on a game originally released in 2004, MTA:SA has remained remarkably active into 2026, with community-reported daily concurrent player counts reportedly reaching into the tens of thousands, rivaling many modern commercially published multiplayer titles, largely due to its scripting flexibility and the strength of its roleplay and custom game-mode server communities. Its target audience is players and modders nostalgic for or newly discovering GTA: San Andreas, as well as scripters and server administrators who want to build custom multiplayer game modes on established game assets. Its closest comparables in the GTA modding space are SA-MP (San Andreas Multiplayer) and RAGE Multiplayer/FiveM (for later Grand Theft Auto titles), with MTA:SA differentiating through its fully open-source codebase, its long, continuous development history dating to the mid-2000s, and its mature Lua scripting ecosystem.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Multi Theft Auto: San Andreas (MTA:SA) is a free, open-source multiplayer modification that adds networked online play to Grand Theft Auto: San Andreas, a single-player game originally released in 2004. Developed and maintained by the volunteer MTA Team, the project's roots trace back to 2003, when an early two-player GTA III multiplayer prototype first demonstrated the concept.

Early playable San Andreas content appeared in January 2006, and the project has since been fully open-sourced under the GPLv3 license, with development continuing publicly on GitHub. More than two decades after its origins, MTA:SA remains actively played, with reports of tens of thousands of concurrent players across its many community-run servers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At its core, MTA:SA acts as a game engine that injects stable networked multiplayer into GTA: San Andreas, supporting up to hundreds of players on a single server. Its defining feature is deep Lua scripting support, which lets server owners build entirely custom game modes, from realistic roleplay cities to racing leagues and deathmatch arenas, on top of the shared engine.

Because MTA:SA is open source and non-commercial, its community has produced an extensive ecosystem of custom maps, resources, and scripts that server administrators can freely install and modify, giving the platform a level of customization rarely found in commercially maintained multiplayer games.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: MTA:SA is completely free to download and play, distributed under the GPLv3 open-source license with no purchase price, subscription, or in-app monetization from the MTA Team itself. Players do need a legally owned copy of the base game, Grand Theft Auto: San Andreas, to run it.

Individual community servers may implement their own optional donation or perk systems, but the core MTA:SA software and official infrastructure carry no cost and are maintained by volunteer developers.

features.title: Networked Multiplayer Engine
features.description: Transforms the single-player GTA: San Andreas into a stable online multiplayer experience supporting large player counts per server.

features.title: Lua Scripting
features.description: Server owners can write custom game modes, rules, and mechanics using the Lua scripting language.

features.title: Custom Game Modes
features.description: Community servers run wildly different experiences, from roleplay and racing to deathmatch and freeroam, on the same engine.

features.title: Open-Source Codebase
features.description: The full engine source code is publicly available on GitHub under the GPLv3 license.

features.title: Custom Maps and Resources
features.description: A large community-built library of maps, vehicles, and scripted resources can be freely added to any server.

features.title: Voice Chat and Chat System
features.description: Built-in communication tools support in-game voice and text chat between players.

features.title: Server Browser
features.description: In-client server browser lets players discover and join the thousands of independently run community servers.

features.title: Non-Commercial, Community-Run
features.description: No official monetization from the MTA Team; the project is sustained entirely by volunteer development and community server operators.

pros: Completely free and open source with no purchase price or subscriptions
Deep Lua scripting enables highly varied, custom game modes across servers
Long continuous development history and an active community after two decades
Large library of community-made maps and scripted resources available
Strong roleplay server ecosystem that has kept the mod culturally relevant
cons: Requires a legally owned copy of the original GTA: San Andreas game to play
Built on a two-decade-old game engine, so visuals and physics feel dated compared to modern titles
Experience varies widely by server since content and rules are entirely community-managed
No official company support; help comes from volunteer community and forums
Setting up or scripting a custom server has a learning curve for newcomers

faqs.question: Is MTA:SA free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Multi Theft Auto: San Andreas is completely free and open source, though you need to own a legal copy of Grand Theft Auto: San Andreas to play it.

faqs.question: What game does MTA:SA modify?
faqs.answer: MTA:SA adds multiplayer functionality to Grand Theft Auto: San Andreas, the 2004 Rockstar North game.

faqs.question: How do MTA:SA servers create custom game modes?
faqs.answer: Server owners use the Lua scripting language to build custom rules, mechanics, and entire game modes on top of the core MTA:SA engine.

faqs.question: Is MTA:SA still active?
faqs.answer: Yes, MTA:SA has remained actively played for over two decades, with community reports of tens of thousands of concurrent players across its servers.

faqs.question: Is MTA:SA source code publicly available?
faqs.answer: Yes, MTA:SA's source code is open source under the GPLv3 license and hosted publicly on GitHub under the multitheftauto organization.

faqs.question: How is MTA:SA different from SA-MP?
faqs.answer: Both are multiplayer mods for GTA: San Andreas, but MTA:SA is fully open source and uses Lua scripting, giving it a different technical foundation and community ecosystem than SA-MP.

use_cases.title: Roleplay Servers
use_cases.description: Communities run heavily scripted roleplay servers simulating jobs, economies, and law enforcement systems within the game world.

use_cases.title: Racing and Minigame Servers
use_cases.description: Server owners create dedicated racing leagues and minigame modes using custom maps and Lua scripts.

use_cases.title: Modding and Scripting Practice
use_cases.description: Aspiring game developers use MTA:SA's Lua scripting environment to learn game scripting and build custom multiplayer mechanics.

features: Full multiplayer client and server software
Access to community maps and resources

enrichment.short_description: multi-scrobbler is an open-source, self-hosted tool that captures listening activity from multiple music sources like Spotify, Plex, and Jellyfin and scrobbles it to Last.fm, ListenBrainz, and Maloja.
enrichment.seo_title: multi-scrobbler Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: multi-scrobbler review 2026: how this open-source, self-hosted scrobbling tool syncs listening history to Last.fm and ListenBrainz, plus setup and features.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: multi-scrobbler is a free, open-source, self-hosted application created and maintained by developer FoxxMD, distributed on GitHub under the MIT license with documentation hosted at foxxmd.github.io/multi-scrobbler. Its purpose is to solve music scrobble-tracking fragmentation: rather than needing a separate scrobbling app or browser extension for every platform someone listens to music on, multi-scrobbler runs as a single background service (most commonly deployed via Docker, though it can also run locally with Node.js) that connects directly to the APIs of multiple listening Sources and multiple scrobble Clients simultaneously. Supported Sources, the places music-listening activity is captured from, include services like Spotify, Plex, Jellyfin, Navidrome, YouTube Music, and existing Last.fm or ListenBrainz histories, among others; supported Clients, the destinations scrobbles are sent to, include Last.fm, ListenBrainz, and the self-hosted scrobble tracker Maloja. Because it talks to service APIs directly instead of relying on per-device or per-app plugins, multi-scrobbler captures listening activity regardless of which device or app was used to play the music, and its self-hosted, open-source nature means users retain full control and visibility over how their listening data is handled rather than trusting a third-party cloud service. The application includes a built-in web dashboard for viewing scrobble status, logs, and system health, and supports monitoring integrations including webhook notifications (Gotify, Ntfy, Apprise), healthcheck endpoints, and Prometheus metrics for users who want to track uptime in their own monitoring stack. It can also enhance or correct scrobble metadata using search-based pattern matching, built-in correction rules, or lookups against the MusicBrainz database, improving accuracy for cases like clean vs. explicit track titles or inconsistent artist naming. As with most projects in the self-hosted, single-maintainer open-source category, there is no company, headquarters, or funding round associated with multi-scrobbler; it is free to deploy and run indefinitely with no subscription or paid tier, and is commonly found within the broader self-hosted media stack alongside tools like Plex, Jellyfin, and Navidrome via platforms like Unraid and Docker Compose templates. Its target audience is music enthusiasts within the self-hosting and home media server community who want unified, accurate, and privately-controlled scrobbling across every app and device they use, especially those who run self-hosted music servers like Jellyfin or Navidrome that lack native Last.fm/ListenBrainz scrobbling of their own. Its closest comparables and competitors are native scrobbling plugins built into individual apps (such as a Jellyfin scrobbler plugin or a Plex Last.fm agent) and other cross-platform scrobbling tools, with multi-scrobbler differentiating by unifying many sources and destinations in one self-hosted service instead of requiring a separate integration per app.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: multi-scrobbler is an open-source, self-hosted tool built by developer FoxxMD to solve a common problem for people who listen to music across many different apps and devices: keeping a single, accurate scrobble history on services like Last.fm or ListenBrainz. Instead of installing a separate scrobbling plugin for every player, multi-scrobbler runs as one background service that talks directly to the APIs of each listening source.

The project is commonly deployed via Docker and is popular within the self-hosted home media community, often running alongside servers like Jellyfin, Plex, or Navidrome to fill in scrobbling functionality those platforms lack natively.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: multi-scrobbler connects to a range of Sources, including Spotify, Plex, Jellyfin, Navidrome, and YouTube Music, and forwards listening activity to Clients such as Last.fm, ListenBrainz, and the self-hosted Maloja scrobble tracker. Because it talks directly to service APIs, it captures plays regardless of which app or device was used.

The tool includes a web dashboard for reviewing scrobble history, status, and logs, plus monitoring options like webhook alerts, healthcheck endpoints, and Prometheus metrics. It can also clean up and correct scrobble metadata using pattern matching or MusicBrainz lookups for more accurate artist and track data.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: multi-scrobbler is completely free and open-source software released under the MIT license, with no paid tiers or subscription plans. Users self-host it on their own hardware or server, most commonly via a Docker container.
As a single-maintainer open-source project, development is community and volunteer-driven rather than commercially funded, and there is no official company or paid support offering behind it.

features.title: Multi-Source Scrobble Capture
features.description: Connects directly to APIs of sources like Spotify, Plex, Jellyfin, Navidrome, and YouTube Music to capture listening activity.

features.title: Multi-Client Scrobbling
features.description: Sends captured scrobbles to multiple destinations simultaneously, including Last.fm, ListenBrainz, and Maloja.

features.title: Web Dashboard
features.description: A built-in interface shows scrobble status, history, and system logs for monitoring activity.

features.title: Monitoring Integrations
features.description: Supports webhook notifications via Gotify, Ntfy, and Apprise, plus healthcheck endpoints and Prometheus metrics.

features.title: Scrobble Metadata Correction
features.description: Cleans up and corrects track and artist metadata using pattern matching or MusicBrainz database lookups.

features.title: Docker Deployment
features.description: Ships as a Docker image for easy self-hosted deployment on x86 and ARM systems, alongside a Node.js install option.

features.title: Direct API Integration
features.description: Communicates directly with service APIs rather than relying on per-app plugins, capturing plays across any device.

features.title: Self-Hosted Data Control
features.description: Keeps listening data under the user's own control since the tool and its logs run entirely on infrastructure the user manages.

pros: Unifies scrobbling from many different music sources into one self-hosted tool
Free and open source under the MIT license with no subscription cost
Direct API integration means it captures listening regardless of the device or app used
Built-in dashboard and monitoring integrations make it easy to verify it is working correctly
Metadata correction via MusicBrainz improves scrobble accuracy for messy track data
cons: Requires self-hosting knowledge and infrastructure such as Docker to deploy
No official company support; help comes from GitHub issues and community documentation
Initial configuration of multiple sources and clients can require careful API credential setup
Smaller user base than mainstream scrobbling apps means fewer third-party tutorials
Depends on the continued availability and API stability of each connected music service

faqs.question: What does multi-scrobbler do?
faqs.answer: multi-scrobbler is a self-hosted tool that captures your music listening activity from multiple sources like Spotify, Plex, and Jellyfin and scrobbles it to services like Last.fm and ListenBrainz.

faqs.question: Is multi-scrobbler free?
faqs.answer: Yes, multi-scrobbler is free and open-source software released under the MIT license, with no paid tiers.

faqs.question: How is multi-scrobbler deployed?
faqs.answer: It is most commonly deployed as a Docker container, though it can also be run locally using Node.js.

faqs.question: What services can multi-scrobbler scrobble to?
faqs.answer: It can send scrobbles to Last.fm, ListenBrainz, and the self-hosted Maloja scrobble tracker.

faqs.question: Why would I use multi-scrobbler instead of a single app's built-in scrobbler?
faqs.answer: multi-scrobbler consolidates scrobbling from many different sources and apps into one place, so you do not need a separate plugin or extension for every platform you listen on.

faqs.question: Who maintains multi-scrobbler?
faqs.answer: multi-scrobbler is developed and maintained by FoxxMD as an open-source project on GitHub.

use_cases.title: Self-Hosted Media Server Scrobbling
use_cases.description: Users running Jellyfin, Plex, or Navidrome deploy multi-scrobbler to add Last.fm and ListenBrainz scrobbling that those servers lack natively.

use_cases.title: Cross-Platform Listening History
use_cases.description: Music listeners who use multiple apps and devices run multi-scrobbler to maintain one unified, accurate scrobble history.

use_cases.title: Migrating Between Scrobble Services
use_cases.description: Users forward existing Last.fm or ListenBrainz history through multi-scrobbler to populate a new scrobble service without losing data.
use_cases.audience: Users switching or backing up scrobble platforms
